FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD), N=7514, ages 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018), 21 research sites across U.S.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort analysis (Years 1-3) using modified Poisson regression to estimate associations between social media time (Year 1) and alcohol sipping (Year 3), with problematic social media use (Year 2) tested as mediator.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original empirical publication in Substance Use & Misuse journal.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Qualifying SM-specific estimate found. "Social media time (Year 1) was prospectively associated with 1.31 (95% confidence interval 1.20-1.43) times higher risk of new-onset sipping (Year 3)." Other side is alcohol sipping (substance use, individual-trait outcome).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: RR=1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43) for social media time; RR=1.03 (95% CI 1.03-1.05) for problematic social media use; indirect effect B=0.011 (95% BC CI 0.007-0.016) with 25.0% mediation (p=0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Direct and indirect effects quantified with 95% confidence intervals and p-values across three models; mediation analysis with bias-corrected bootstrap confidence intervals.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media appears as IV (independent variable) in main model (social media time → sipping). Although design is longitudinal and sipping is DV, SM-as-IV analysis exists, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong prospective evidence for SM time associated with increased alcohol sipping, partially mediated by problematic SM use.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=4,718 adolescents aged 10-15 years, 2018-2021, 21 demographic sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort analysis (Year 2-3) examining joint associations of screen time and physical activity (steps per day) with CVD risk factors using multivariate linear and logistic regression.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original empirical publication in BMC Public Health.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific isolated estimates found. Step 2: Composite check. Screen time composite includes "television shows, movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, social media, and texting." This bundles non-phone/non-SM items (TV, movies, unspecified gaming) with phone/SM items (texting, video chat, social media) without separate per-item breakouts in primary results tables. Composite with non-phone ingredient without per-device breakdown → NOT_MET per binding clarification on multi-device aggregation.

notes: Paper analyzes broad "recreational screen time" aggregate without isolating phone/SM components in primary analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=9,538 adolescents (9-10 years at baseline 2016-2018) with 2 years follow-up, 21 recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort analysis (baseline to Year 2 follow-up) using mixed-effects models to examine associations between baseline self-reported screen time and parent-reported mental health symptoms on Child Behavior Checklist.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original empirical publication in BMC Public Health.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No isolated phone/SM-specific coefficient found in primary results. Main results table (Table 2) reports only continuous aggregate "total screen time" (B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06, 0.13) and categorical screen time (0 to 4 h, >4-8, >8 h). Text mentions "video chat, texting, videos, and video games" had greatest associations, but these modality-specific estimates are not reported with separate coefficients in primary analyses. Step 2: Composite structure includes TV, movies, video streaming (non-phone), video chat, texting, video games (platform unspecified), and social media as aggregate. "Videos" and "video games" without platform specification fail criteria (gaming alone without explicit phone/mobile platform specification fails FT-IC2). Composite bundles non-phone/non-SM (TV, movies, unspecified gaming) with phone/SM without per-item breakout → NOT_MET.

notes: Paper examines aggregate screen time composite including TV and unspecified gaming without isolation of phone/SM modality in primary analyses.
